key_departures.impact: Released on the first day of the new league year. Cutting Cousins frees over $2M in cap space but adds $22.5M in dead money. Was the incumbent starter but underperformed badly; his departure was expected and necessary for the new regime.

Elite blitzing linebacker who recorded 107 tackles, 3.5 sacks, and 1 INT in 17 games in 2025. Defensive coordinator Jeff Ulbrich said replacing him 'would take more than one human being.' His departure to a division rival creates a massive hole at the starter LB spot.

key_departures.new_team: Buffalo Bills (3yr/$15.75M base / up to $21M, $10.12M guaranteed)
key_departures.impact: Versatile slot/boundary corner who had a breakout 2025 with 3 INTs, 13 PDs, and 2 sacks. Was the team's most important secondary utility player. Departure is a meaningful secondary loss — both as a starter and a special teamer.

Started all 17 games in 2025 with 62 tackles and a 78.2 PFF grade. Was Atlanta's primary interior run-stopper. Departure leaves the IDL thin against the run, which was already a weakness (ranked 27th in EPA per rush allowed in 2025).
Reliable short-yardage back and complementary piece to Bijan Robinson. Had 8 rushing TDs in 2025. Falcons still have Robinson and are unlikely to add at RB early, but the RB2 role is now vacant.

Released before free agency began. Was set to carry an $18.4M cap hit in 2026. Had an injury-marred, forgettable 2025 after a terrific 2024. Departure was cap-driven but further depleted an already thin WR room.
Released to save $2.64M in cap space. Was a special teams contributor and WR depth piece. Loss is minimal from an on-field standpoint but further thins the receiver room.

Former second-round pick who accumulated 16.5 sacks over four years with the Falcons but never fully realized his potential. With James Pearce Jr. facing felony charges and potential suspension, losing Ebiketie further thins the edge group.
Veteran rotational edge rusher. Departure contributes to pass-rush depth concern alongside the uncertainty around James Pearce Jr.

signings.analysis: The Dolphins absorbed a record ~$100M dead cap hit to release Tagovailoa, making him available at the veteran minimum due to offset language. He will likely be the Week 1 starter given Michael Penix Jr.'s ACL recovery timeline. This is a bridge deal, not a long-term solution — it is firmly a one-year stopgap. Minimum contract signals pure bridge. QB remains a critical need long-term (Penix is the future, but his return timeline is unclear), but the immediate starter hole is patched. Need partially addressed for 2026 only.

Former 16th overall pick who underwhelmed in Washington and Philadelphia (37 catches, 478 yds in 2 Eagles seasons). Atlanta is betting on a change of scenery and better quarterback play unlocking his upside. $7.5M AAV is below the WR starter market, reflecting the risk. Two years with $10M guaranteed shows moderate commitment — this is a WR2/3 addition, not a true WR2 solution. The Falcons receiver room remains a critical need: Drake London is the only proven option. Dotson + Zaccheaus gives depth but not an established WR2. Still a pressing draft need.

Reunion signing — Zaccheaus spent his first four years in Atlanta as a 2019 UDFA. Returns as a WR3/4 depth piece. Terms not officially disclosed but reported as a low-cost deal consistent with his recent market. Provides familiarity with the organization and slot depth. Does not address the WR2 void left by Mooney's release. WR remains a top-tier draft need.

Another reunion signing. Hooper was a Falcon from 2016-2019 and is familiar to president of football Matt Ryan. HC Kevin Stefanski loves 12-personnel. Pairs with franchise-tagged Kyle Pitts as a TE2 blocker/receiver. One-year deal at $3.25M is pure depth/role-player money. TE was not a pre-FA critical need given Pitts's tag, and this signing keeps it off the board entirely.

Franchise tagged at $15.045M (fully guaranteed). Pitts had a breakout 2025 with 88 catches, 928 yards, and 5 TDs — career highs across the board. The tag keeps Atlanta's most dynamic pass catcher on the roster for 2026. Long-term extension talks will continue through the July 15 deadline. TE is not a draft need with Pitts tagged and Hooper added as TE2.

Former Cardinals 3rd-round pick (2022) who spent last 1.5 seasons with the Browns under Kevin Stefanski. Posted 2.5 sacks and 13 pressures in 300+ defensive snaps in 2025. Versatile inside/outside rusher with upside, but clearly a rotational/depth signing at $3.1M. Does NOT solve the EDGE pass-rush need — especially with James Pearce Jr. facing potential suspension and both Ebiketie and Floyd departed. EDGE remains a pressing need.

Former Texans 3rd-round pick (No. 75 overall, 2022) who has battled injuries — played just 3 games in 2024 and saw only 10% defensive snap share in 2025 (42% special teams). Terms not officially disclosed; reported as a low-cost deal. Joins a LB room of Divine Deablo, JD Bertrand, and Troy Andersen (restructured). Harris is a developmental bet — a Deablo-type project — but nowhere near replacing Kaden Elliss ($33M/3yr at Saints). LB remains a critical tier-1 need. The Falcons need a proven starter opposite Deablo.

The Falcons used three different kickers in 2025 (Koo, Romo, Gonzalez). Folk was the NFL's most accurate kicker over the last three seasons, making 96.6% of his field goals in 2025. Two-year deal provides genuine stability. K is no longer a need.

All-Pro punter in 2020 with the Patriots. Atlanta is revamping a historically poor special teams unit. Three years and $5M guaranteed is a real investment in the punting position. P is a fully addressed need.
UDFA extended by the Falcons. Has appeared in 26 games but made only one start. Pure depth/practice squad-level CB who does not move the needle on the CB need created by Dee Alford's departure.
Exclusive rights free agent tendered by the Falcons. Signed off the Giants' practice squad mid-2025 season. Roster retention move only — does not meaningfully address EDGE pass-rush need.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Tua Tagovailoa (1yr/$1.3M) bridges the 2026 season while Michael Penix Jr. recovers from his ACL injury. Tua is likely the Week 1 starter. However, this is explicitly a bridge deal at the veteran minimum — QB remains a tier-2 need for the draft since Penix's return timeline is uncertain and his long-term viability as a franchise QB remains unproven. Atlanta should still consider a QB with a mid-round pick if a worthy prospect falls.

The Falcons added Jahan Dotson (2yr/$15M) and Olamide Zaccheaus (depth), but this room is still critically deficient. Drake London is the only proven WR1-caliber option. Dotson is a WR2/3 project with a poor track record; Zaccheaus is WR4 depth. The team cut Darnell Mooney and KhaDarel Hodge. WR remains a top-tier need — Atlanta must add a quality WR2 in the draft.

The Falcons lost Kaden Elliss (3yr/$33M, $23M gtd to Saints) — their best defensive player and a true three-down linebacker. DC Ulbrich said replacing him would 'take more than one human being.' The only replacement signed is Christian Harris on a cheap depth deal. Troy Andersen is injury-prone and Divine Deablo is more of a hybrid/safety. LB is now a critical tier-1 need and the most urgent remaining hole on the roster.

Dee Alford's departure (3yr/$21M to Bills) removes the team's most versatile secondary player. Natrone Brooks (1 career start) was re-signed as depth. The Falcons have Cobee Bryant, Clark Phillips III, and Billy Bowman Jr. (recovering from Achilles) but lack a proven starter opposite A.J. Terrell Jr. CB remains a tier-2 need that should be addressed via the draft or continued FA activity.

The Falcons lost both Arnold Ebiketie and Leonard Floyd to free agency. James Pearce Jr. is facing five felony charges and a likely suspension, making him an unreliable projection. Jalon Walker is promising but unproven as a lead rusher. The only additions are Cameron Thomas (1yr/$3.1M, rotational) and Elijah Garcia (ERFA minimum). The edge room is dangerously thin and EDGE has escalated to a tier-1 draft priority.

David Onyemata (1yr/$10.5M to Jets) was Atlanta's primary interior run-stopper. The Falcons finished 27th in EPA per rush allowed in 2025. No IDL additions have been confirmed in free agency. Ruke Orhorhoro and Brandon Dorlus are internal options but have not proven they can anchor the position. IDL remains a tier-2 need that should be addressed in the mid-rounds of the draft.

Tyler Allgeier (2yr/$12.25M to Cardinals) departed, removing the team's primary backup to Bijan Robinson and their red-zone short-yardage specialist. Robinson is elite and under contract, but the Falcons need a capable RB2. This can be addressed late in the draft or through continued FA additions, but it is a real gap.

Kyle Pitts franchise-tagged ($15.045M guaranteed) and Austin Hooper added as TE2 (1yr/$3.25M). Stefanski's 12-personnel heavy offense is well-stocked at the position. TE drops from a need to a luxury/developmental pick at best.

No IOL signings or departures of note. Chris Lindstrom anchors the interior. Ryan Neuzil and Matthew Bergeron (guard eligible) are depth. No movement here — need unchanged at tier-3, addressable in mid-to-late rounds.

Jake Matthews remains the veteran LT but is aging. Kaleb McGary is the RT. No additions or losses at the position. Need unchanged — a developmental OT in the mid-rounds remains valuable but not urgent.

key_additions.headline: Former Pro Bowl QB serves as bridge starter while Michael Penix Jr. recovers from ACL tear
Former 16th overall pick gets fresh start as Falcons' WR2/3, betting on upside in new system
Breakout TE retained on franchise tag after career year (88 rec, 928 yds, 5 TDs); long-term extension talks ongoing
Veteran TE reunites with president Matt Ryan, pairing with Pitts in Stefanski's 12-personnel heavy offense
Reunites with coach Stefanski; adds rotational pass-rush depth to a thin edge group
Athletic former 3rd-round pick gets opportunity to replace Kaden Elliss opposite Divine Deablo
NFL's most accurate kicker over last 3 seasons ends Atlanta's revolving-door kicker carousel
2020 All-Pro punter overhauling a special teams unit ranked among the NFL's worst in 2025
2019 UDFA fan favorite returns to Atlanta as WR3/4 depth piece in rebuilt receiver room

key_losses.headline: Best defensive player departs to division rival; DC Ulbrich said replacing him would 'take more than one human being'

key_losses.contract_summary: 3yr/$15.75M base / up to $21M ($10.12M gtd) with Bills
key_losses.headline: Breakout utility corner (3 INTs, 13 PDs in 2025) leaves Atlanta's secondary noticeably thinner
Primary interior run-stopper departs; Falcons already ranked 27th in EPA per rush allowed in 2025
Short-yardage specialist and Bijan Robinson's primary complement leaves; RB2 role now open
Failed $180M experiment ends as expected; Cousins cut to facilitate new regime's QB plan
Cap casualty after injury-marred 2025; departure further thins an already depleted WR room
Former 2nd-round pick departs amid James Pearce Jr. legal uncertainty, leaving edge room dangerously thin
